summary: I live in Lexington, Kentucky, and I really appreciate the beautiful environment here, especially living on what used to be a horse farm. I worked as the first environmental commissioner for the City of Lexington, starting right when the city went under an EPA consent decree in the early 2000s due to unmanaged storm water issues. It was a rewarding job because I could see the direct impact of environmental initiatives at the local level, like improving sewer systems and starting recycling programs.

entity: Kentucky Climate Change Oral History project
summary: This interview is part of the Kentucky Climate Change Oral History project, which I participated in on July 10th, 2023. The project is important for documenting environmental experiences and histories in Kentucky, and I was happy to share my story and work related to environmental protection and policy in the state. It was conducted by Zada Komara, a faculty member at the University of Kentucky.

I attended the University of Kentucky after starting college at the University of Tennessee because I preferred UK’s blue colors over orange. I earned my first degree in social work there and later returned to get a mechanical engineering degree. I also worked for UK managing hospital maintenance planning, which gave me insight into hospital environments and waste streams.

I was actually born in Laurel County, Kentucky, because there was no hospital facility in Clay County at the time. Although I didn't grow up there, it’s where my life began before moving to Manchester and Clay County. My family roots are deeply connected to the coal communities in that region.

I lived as a child in Clay County, Kentucky, specifically in Manchester, where my dad worked for the local coal company. Growing up there was tough as the coal industry started to decline in the 1960s, which eventually led my family to move to Louisville. Clay County was a coal mining community, and many of my ancestors were coal miners or farmers in that area.

Manchester, Kentucky, is where I spent my childhood in Clay County. My father worked for the local coal company there, and it was a typical coal community. The decline of the coal industry in the 1960s made work scarce, which eventually caused my family to relocate to Louisville for better opportunities.

My father was a World War II veteran, and after the war, he used the GI Bill to attend college. His military service and education helped him become an accountant for the coal mine where he worked. His experience shaped much of our family’s story and opportunities.

The GI Bill was instrumental for my father after World War II, allowing him to go to college and become an accountant for the coal mine. It provided educational opportunities that were crucial for his career transition from military service to civilian life. This support had a lasting impact on our family’s stability.

My father was born in Pineville, Kentucky, and later lived in Harlan. These places were part of the coal communities that shaped his upbringing and work life. Although I was not raised there, these towns are part of my family’s coal mining heritage.

Harlan, Kentucky, is where my father lived after being born in Pineville. It’s another coal mining town that was central to my family’s history. These communities influenced my early understanding of the coal industry and its decline.

When the coal industry declined in the 1960s, my family moved from Manchester to Louisville, Kentucky, which was a big culture shock for me. I struggled academically in the Jefferson County school system because I wasn’t prepared for the jump. We lived in Louisville until I was in high school before moving again to Knoxville, Tennessee.

I attended school in Jefferson County, Kentucky, after moving there from Clay County. It was challenging because I wasn’t academically ready for the school system there. This period was a significant transition in my education and life before moving to Knoxville for high school.

My family moved to Knoxville, Tennessee, where I finished high school. I started college early at the University of Tennessee but quickly transferred back to the University of Kentucky because I preferred it. Knoxville was an important place for my education and personal growth.

I began my college education at the University of Tennessee in Knoxville, starting a year early. However, I transferred to the University of Kentucky soon after because I liked UK better. My time at UT was brief but part of my educational journey.

I worked for Procter & Gamble for almost 24 years right out of college, mostly in manufacturing roles like engineering, operations, and maintenance. Mid-career, I took on environmental permitting and safety programs, which I really enjoyed because it combined my social work and engineering backgrounds. Procter & Gamble was supportive, promoting women and providing extensive training and certifications in environmental areas.

While working at Procter & Gamble, we celebrated Earth Day for the first time at the company. It was part of our new environmental initiatives like recycling and planting trees. These efforts were pioneering for the company back then and made me proud to be part of the environmental progress.

Lloyd Cress was the Kentucky state commissioner for the environment when I joined as a special projects manager and technical advisor. I loved working with him and in the public sector, helping with education, regulations, and programs like Kentucky EXCEL. When Lloyd was promoted, I was honored to be promoted into his role as commissioner, becoming the first female commissioner for Kentucky’s environment.

Kentucky EXCEL was a voluntary leadership program we started under my time working with Lloyd Cress to promote environmentally conscientious behavior. It was designed for people who wanted to lead in environmental stewardship and compliance. I was proud to help launch this program as part of our broader environmental education efforts.

Kentucky is my home state where I was born and spent much of my life and career. From my early days in coal communities to working as the state environmental commissioner, I have been deeply involved in Kentucky’s environmental issues. Representing Kentucky on a national level and working with EPA Region 4 was a significant part of my career.

As Kentucky’s environmental commissioner, I regularly met with EPA Region 4 leaders, which includes several southern states. These meetings were eye-opening and showed me how much opportunity there is to improve environmental conditions through cooperation. It was an important part of representing Kentucky on the national environmental stage.

Ernie Fletcher was the Republican governor I worked for as Kentucky’s environmental commissioner. Despite our different political views—I'm a liberal Democrat—he supported many environmental initiatives and judged my work on its merits, which I appreciated. His leadership allowed me to focus on environmental progress without partisan interference.

Jim Newberry hired me to be the first environmental commissioner for the City of Lexington after Governor Fletcher’s term ended. He was a magnificent person to work for, and I valued the opportunity to impact local environmental policies directly. Working under his leadership was one of the finest jobs I ever had.

I served as the first environmental commissioner for the City of Lexington, starting during a challenging time when the city was under an EPA consent decree due to storm water management failures. My role involved bringing the city into compliance, improving sewer systems, launching recycling programs, and implementing energy and air quality initiatives. It was rewarding to see tangible improvements in Fayette County through my work.

The City of Lexington went under an EPA consent decree in the early 2000s after two young women drowned due to unmanaged storm water near the football stadium. I took over as environmental commissioner at that time to help the city comply with federal law and improve storm water and sewer systems. The decree pushed us to develop proper maps and infrastructure to prevent such tragedies and environmental hazards.

Fayette County is where Lexington is located, and during my time as environmental commissioner, we implemented the first energy and air programs there. We also bought new garbage trucks and started a recycling program to improve waste management. It was fulfilling to contribute to environmental improvements in Fayette County.

Jim Gray succeeded Jim Newberry as mayor of Lexington, and while I stayed on for a while, he was not very supportive of environmental initiatives. This lack of support influenced my decision to leave the city job. Despite that, I appreciated the opportunities I had under previous leadership.

After my time with the City of Lexington, I worked for Kentucky American Water, among other roles. This position allowed me to continue applying my environmental and safety expertise in the private sector. It was one of several diverse jobs I held before retiring.

I worked at Wild Turkey Distillery managing environmental and safety programs. This role was part of my later career where I helped businesses comply with environmental laws and improve sustainability practices. It was another opportunity to make a positive impact in Kentucky’s industrial sector.

I retired just two weeks before the COVID-19 pandemic shut everything down, which was unfortunate timing because I was bored during the early months. After a couple of months, I returned to work for the state in the enforcement division, mostly working remotely. The pandemic shaped how we did environmental enforcement during that time.

London, Kentucky, is one of the locations where the state has a field office for environmental inspectors. During my time in state environmental enforcement, offices like the one in London housed inspectors who monitored compliance with air, waste, and water regulations. It’s part of the decentralized system Kentucky uses to manage environmental programs.

Rockwell International was mentioned as a private engineering company based in Cincinnati where I did environmental consulting after working at UK. I didn’t enjoy that kind of consulting work as much, so I moved on to other roles. It was part of my varied career in environmental fields.

Paducah, Kentucky, was not directly discussed in detail, but it is one of the locations where Kentucky’s environmental programs have field offices. These offices support the state’s efforts to monitor and enforce environmental regulations across different regions.
